concepts.name: principal curves
concepts.description: Smooth one-dimensional curves that pass through the middle of a distribution, generalizing principal components to nonlinear manifolds.
concepts.existing_quote: Principal curves generalize the principal component line, providing a smooth one-dimensional curved approximation to a set of data points in IRp.

paragraphs.text:
This is page 485
Printer: Opaque this
14
Unsupervised Learning

The previous chapters have been concerned with predicting the values
of one or more outputs or response variables Y
= (Y1, . . . , Ym) for a
given set of input or predictor variables XT = (X1, . . . , Xp). Denote by
xT
i
= (xi1, . . . , xip) the inputs for the ith training case, and let yi be a
response measurement. The predictions are based on the training sample
(x1, y1), . . . , (xN, yN) of previously solved cases, where the joint values of
all of the variables are known. This is called supervised learning or “learn-
ing with a teacher.” Under this metaphor the “student” presents an an-
swer ˆyi for each xi in the training sample, and the supervisor or “teacher”
provides either the correct answer and/or an error associated with the stu-
dent’s answer. This is usually characterized by some loss function L(y, ˆy),
for example, L(y, ˆy) = (y −ˆy)2.
If one supposes that (X, Y ) are random variables represented by some
joint probability density Pr(X, Y ), then supervised learning can be formally
characterized as a density estimation problem where one is concerned with
determining properties of the conditional density Pr(Y |X). Usually the
properties of interest are the “location” parameters μ that minimize the
expected error at each x,

where Pr(X) is the joint marginal density of the X values alone. In su-
pervised learning Pr(X) is typically of no direct concern. One is interested
mainly in the properties of the conditional density Pr(Y |X). Since Y is of-
ten of low dimension (usually one), and only its location μ(x) is of interest,
the problem is greatly simpliﬁed. As discussed in the previous chapters,
there are many approaches for successfully addressing supervised learning
in a variety of contexts.
In this chapter we address unsupervised learning or “learning without a
teacher.” In this case one has a set of N observations (x1, x2, . . . , xN) of a
random p-vector X having joint density Pr(X). The goal is to directly infer
the properties of this probability density without the help of a supervisor or
teacher providing correct answers or degree-of-error for each observation.
The dimension of X is sometimes much higher than in supervised learn-
ing, and the properties of interest are often more complicated than simple
location estimates. These factors are somewhat mitigated by the fact that
X represents all of the variables under consideration; one is not required
to infer how the properties of Pr(X) change, conditioned on the changing
values of another set of variables.
In low-dimensional problems (say p ≤3), there are a variety of eﬀective
nonparametric methods for directly estimating the density Pr(X) itself at
all X-values, and representing it graphically (Silverman, 1986, e.g.). Owing
to the curse of dimensionality, these methods fail in high dimensions. One
must settle for estimating rather crude global models, such as Gaussian
mixtures or various simple descriptive statistics that characterize Pr(X).
Generally, these descriptive statistics attempt to characterize X-values,
or collections of such values, where Pr(X) is relatively large. Principal
components, multidimensional scaling, self-organizing maps, and principal
curves, for example, attempt to identify low-dimensional manifolds within
the X-space that represent high data density. This provides information
about the associations among the variables and whether or not they can be
considered as functions of a smaller set of “latent” variables. Cluster anal-
ysis attempts to ﬁnd multiple convex regions of the X-space that contain
modes of Pr(X). This can tell whether or not Pr(X) can be represented by
a mixture of simpler densities representing distinct types or classes of ob-
servations. Mixture modeling has a similar goal. Association rules attempt
to construct simple descriptions (conjunctive rules) that describe regions
of high density in the special case of very high dimensional binary-valued
data.
With supervised learning there is a clear measure of success, or lack
thereof, that can be used to judge adequacy in particular situations and
to compare the eﬀectiveness of diﬀerent methods over various situations.

concepts.name: principal surfaces
concepts.description: Smooth two-dimensional surfaces that generalize principal curves to higher-dimensional manifolds.
concepts.existing_quote: Principal surfaces have exactly the same form as principal curves, but are of higher dimension.

concepts.name: kernel density estimation
concepts.description: A nonparametric method for estimating the probability density function by placing kernel functions at each data point.
concepts.existing_quote: Kernel density estimation is a general technique for estimating probability densities from data. A kernel function K_λ is centered at each observation, and the density estimate is the sum (or average) of these kernels.

concepts.name: encoding step in vector quantization
concepts.description: The clustering process in vector quantization where pixel blocks are assigned to their nearest codeword.
concepts.existing_quote: The clustering process is called the encoding step, and the collection of centroids is called the codebook.

concepts.name: rate in bits per pixel
concepts.description: Measure of compression efficiency expressed as log2(K)/4 bits per pixel, where K is the number of codewords.
concepts.existing_quote: This is typically expressed as a rate in bits per pixel: log2(K)/4, which are 1.91 and 0.50, respectively.

concepts.name: fixed-length code
concepts.description: Coding scheme using the same number of bits for each codeword, requiring log2(K) bits to identify K codewords.
concepts.existing_quote: This uses a fixed-length code, and is inefficient if some codewords occur many more times than others in the image.

= (Y1, . . . , Ym) for a
given set of input or predictor variables XT = (X1, . . . , Xp). Denote by
xT
i
= (xi1, . . . , xip) the inputs for the ith training case, and let yi be a
response measurement. The predictions are based on the training sample
(x1, y1), . . . , (xN, yN) of previously solved cases, where the joint values of
all of the variables are known. This is called supervised learning or “learn-
ing with a teacher.” Under this metaphor the “student” presents an an-
swer ˆyi for each xi in the training sample, and the supervisor or “teacher”
provides either the correct answer and/or an error associated with the stu-
dent’s answer. This is usually characterized by some loss function L(y, ˆy),
for example, L(y, ˆy) = (y −ˆy)2.
If one supposes that (X, Y ) are random variables represented by some
joint probability density Pr(X, Y ), then supervised learning can be formally
characterized as a density estimation problem where one is concerned with
determining properties of the conditional density Pr(Y |X). Usually the
properties of interest are the “location” parameters μ that minimize the
expected error at each x,

where Pr(X) is the joint marginal density of the X values alone. In su-

mainly in the properties of the conditional density Pr(Y |X). Since Y is of-
ten of low dimension (usually one), and only its location μ(x) is of interest,
the problem is greatly simpliﬁed. As discussed in the previous chapters,
there are many approaches for successfully addressing supervised learning
in a variety of contexts.

teacher.” In this case one has a set of N observations (x1, x2, . . . , xN) of a
random p-vector X having joint density Pr(X). The goal is to directly infer
the properties of this probability density without the help of a supervisor or
teacher providing correct answers or degree-of-error for each observation.

ing, and the properties of interest are often more complicated than simple
location estimates. These factors are somewhat mitigated by the fact that

must settle for estimating rather crude global models, such as Gaussian
mixtures or various simple descriptive statistics that characterize Pr(X).

or collections of such values, where Pr(X) is relatively large. Principal

curves, for example, attempt to identify low-dimensional manifolds within

servations. Mixture modeling has a similar goal. Association rules attempt
to construct simple descriptions (conjunctive rules) that describe regions
of high density in the special case of very high dimensional binary-valued

thereof, that can be used to judge adequacy in particular situations and
to compare the eﬀectiveness of diﬀerent methods over various situations.

concepts.name: variable length code
concepts.description: Coding scheme using different numbers of bits for different codewords based on frequency, achieving better compression via Shannon coding theory.
concepts.existing_quote: Using Shannon coding theory, we know that in general a variable length code will do better, and the rate then becomes −∑K ℓ=1 pℓlog2(pℓ)/4.

concepts.name: cophenetic dissimilarity
concepts.description: Intergroup dissimilarity at which two observations are first joined together in the same cluster in hierarchical clustering.
concepts.existing_quote: The cophenetic dissimilarity Cii′ between two observations (i, i′) is the intergroup dissimilarity at which observations i and i′ are first joined together in the same cluster.

concepts.name: ultrametric inequality
concepts.description: Constraint on cophenetic dissimilarities requiring Cik ≤ max(Cii′, Ci′k) for any three observations, implying isosceles triangles.
concepts.existing_quote: Also these dissimilarities obey the ultrametric inequality

concepts.name: complete linkage agglomerative clustering
concepts.description: Hierarchical clustering taking intergroup dissimilarity as maximum dissimilarity between groups; produces compact clusters with small diameters.
concepts.existing_quote: Complete linkage (CL) agglomerative clustering (furthest-neighbor technique) takes the intergroup dissimilarity to be that of the furthest (most dissimilar) pair
concepts.existing_formula: d_{CL}(G, H) = \max_{i \in G, i' \in H} d_{ii'}

given set of input or predictor variables XT = (X1, . . . , Xp). Denote by
xT
i
= (xi1, . . . , xip) the inputs for the ith training case, and let yi be a
response measurement. The predictions are based on the training sample
(x1, y1), . . . , (xN, yN) of previously solved cases, where the joint values of
all of the variables are known. This is called supervised learning or “learn-
ing with a teacher.” Under this metaphor the “student” presents an an-
swer ˆyi for each xi in the training sample, and the supervisor or “teacher”
provides either the correct answer and/or an error associated with the stu-
dent’s answer. This is usually characterized by some loss function L(y, ˆy),
for example, L(y, ˆy) = (y −ˆy)2.
If one supposes that (X, Y ) are random variables represented by some
joint probability density Pr(X, Y ), then supervised learning can be formally
characterized as a density estimation problem where one is concerned with
determining properties of the conditional density Pr(Y |X). Usually the
properties of interest are the “location” parameters μ that minimize the
expected error at each x,

where Pr(X) is the joint marginal density of the X values alone. In su-
pervised learning Pr(X) is typically of no direct concern. One is interested
mainly in the properties of the conditional density Pr(Y |X). Since Y is of-
ten of low dimension (usually one), and only its location μ(x) is of interest,

there are many approaches for successfully addressing supervised learning
in a variety of contexts.

teacher.” In this case one has a set of N observations (x1, x2, . . . , xN) of a
random p-vector X having joint density Pr(X). The goal is to directly infer
the properties of this probability density without the help of a supervisor or
teacher providing correct answers or degree-of-error for each observation.
The dimension of X is sometimes much higher than in supervised learn-
ing, and the properties of interest are often more complicated than simple
location estimates. These factors are somewhat mitigated by the fact that

to infer how the properties of Pr(X) change, conditioned on the changing

nonparametric methods for directly estimating the density Pr(X) itself at
all X-values, and representing it graphically (Silverman, 1986, e.g.). Owing
to the curse of dimensionality, these methods fail in high dimensions. One
must settle for estimating rather crude global models, such as Gaussian
mixtures or various simple descriptive statistics that characterize Pr(X).

or collections of such values, where Pr(X) is relatively large. Principal
components, multidimensional scaling, self-organizing maps, and principal
curves, for example, attempt to identify low-dimensional manifolds within

to construct simple descriptions (conjunctive rules) that describe regions

thereof, that can be used to judge adequacy in particular situations and
to compare the eﬀectiveness of diﬀerent methods over various situations.

concepts.name: rank-q linear model
concepts.description: Parametric representation of affine hyperplane f(λ) = μ + Vqλ approximating data points by least squares.
concepts.existing_quote: consider the rank-q linear model for representing them

concepts.name: right singular vectors
concepts.description: Columns vj of matrix V in SVD, defining principal component directions; first q columns solve rank-q approximation problem.
concepts.existing_quote: V is a p×p orthogonal matrix (VT V = Ip) with columns vj called the right singular vectors

concepts.name: singular values
concepts.description: Diagonal elements d1 ≥ d2 ≥ ⋅⋅⋅ ≥ dp ≥ 0 of diagonal matrix D in SVD, measuring variance along principal component directions.
concepts.existing_quote: D is a p×p diagonal matrix, with diagonal elements d1 ≥ d2 ≥ ⋅⋅⋅ ≥ dp ≥ 0 known as the singular values.

concepts.name: mutual k-nearest-neighbor graph
concepts.description: Similarity graph connecting pairs where one point is among K-nearest neighbors of the other, with nonzero edge weights only for neighbors.

concepts.name: kernel principal components
concepts.description: Nonlinear PCA obtained by applying PCA in implicit feature space defined by kernel, expanding scope to non-linear transformations.
concepts.existing_quote: Kernel PCA (Schölkopf et al., 1999) expand the scope of PCA, mimicking what we would obtain if we were to expand the features by non-linear transformations, and then apply PCA in this transformed feature space.
